explicit_text: I was born in Brooklyn on January 2nd, 1898, right at the turn of the century. I also attended Alexander Hamilton High School there, where I drew cartoons for the annuals and bulletin boards. Brooklyn was an important part of my early life and artistic development.

explicit_text: Winsor McCay was a major influence on me, especially his animation work like 'Gertie the Dinosaur.' I learned that he got his ideas from little photographic animation books, similar to the flipbooks I used as a kid. I even reenacted his performance on a Disney TV show, which was a great honor since his work still exists today.

explicit_text: The first film I ever saw was at Coney Island, a film of the Nelson-Ganz fight. It was shown in little restaurants where my family and I would eat hot dogs and watch these strange movies. That experience introduced me to early film and animation, including works by the Lumière brothers.

explicit_text: Wallace Carlson was an animator whose work I saw around 1914 in the Bronx, including 'Dreamy Dud.' He was one of the early pioneers of animated cartoon movies, though not often mentioned today. His films came out about the same time as Winsor McCay's, and they helped shape my early fascination with animation.

explicit_text: I saw 'Gertie the Dinosaur' at the Cretona Theater in the Bronx, and it made a lasting impression on me. entity: Art Students League of New York
explicit_text: I spent about a year at the Art Students League of New York studying under Bridgman, the famous anatomy teacher. Although I studied anatomy and painted a little, I always wanted to be a cartoonist, so I eventually left to pursue animation. The League was an important but brief chapter in my artistic training.

explicit_text: Bridgman was the famous anatomy teacher I studied under at the Art Students League in New York. He was well known for his teaching, though his lessons didn't really rub off on me much. Still, studying anatomy with Bridgman gave me some foundation even though I preferred cartooning.

explicit_text: I lived near Fordham and would often pass a sign on the building opposite the university that said 'Raoul Barre: Cartoonist.' That sign intrigued me so much that I went upstairs and got my first job in animation there around 1916. Fordham was close to where my animation career began.

explicit_text: Raoul Barre was the cartoonist I approached for a job when I was starting out, and he immediately put me to work tracing at his studio. He was a genial Frenchman with a Canadian brogue and ran a small studio of about fifteen people making 'Mutt and Jeff' cartoons. Barre's studio was where I learned the basics of animation and got promoted to animator within weeks.

explicit_text: We sometimes imitated Heinrich Kley's flashy drawing techniques in our animation work at Barre's studio. His style influenced the way we inked drawings on paper before transferring them to celluloid. Kley's work was a source of artistic inspiration for us during those early days.
